summary:
Maine's LD 307 completed final legislative passage on April 14–15, 2026 (House 79-62, Senate 21-13) and now awaits Governor Janet Mills' signature. If signed, it would become the first statewide data center moratorium in US history, banning approvals for facilities requiring 20+ MW until November 2027 and establishing a Data Center Coordination Council. Governor Mills has expressed reservations about the lack of a carve-out for a proposed Jay data center, and Franklin County officials publicly urged a veto on April 21. Her decision — expected imminently — will set a national precedent.

Beyond Maine, the broader pattern holds: state-level moratorium bills are advancing but mostly stalled. New York S9144 (3-year moratorium on 20 MW+ facilities) remains in committee. Vermont S.205 (moratorium through 2030) has advanced only 25% through the originating chamber. Michigan HB 4521 was introduced with bipartisan support but faces opposition from Governor Whitmer. The Sanders-Ocasio-Cortez federal bill (S.4214) was referred to committee in March and is unlikely to advance under current congressional control. Meanwhile local moratoriums continue to proliferate across Indiana, Georgia, Iowa, Colorado, and Illinois, with dozens of counties and municipalities acting independently.

On the construction side, Meta's Hyperion campus in Richland Parish, Louisiana is now actively under construction with approximately 3,700 workers on site as of late March 2026, backed by a $27 billion joint venture with Blue Owl Capital. Ten gas-fired power plants totaling 7.5 GW are planned to power the site via Entergy. Crusoe Energy announced a new 900 MW AI factory campus in Abilene, Texas for Microsoft on March 27, adjacent to the OpenAI Stargate campus where Crusoe recently topped out the final building and full completion is on track for mid-2026.

The national landscape shows a widening gap between the scale of infrastructure investment and the regulatory capacity of state and local governments. While no state has enacted a broad moratorium to date, Maine's bill reaching the governor's desk signals that the political will exists to act. 17 states maintain active incentive programs with no restriction legislation, but active local moratoriums now number in the dozens. The next 30 days — centered on Governor Mills' decision — represent the most consequential moment for data center policy since this wave of legislation began.
